title: 2026 Season Overview: Two Rounds In
caption: Lead image: 2026 season overview
body:
Two rapid-fire races into the 2026 season—one incredibly chaotic wet afternoon in Melbourne, and one tactically intricate sprint weekend under the sun in Shanghai—and the broad shape of this exciting new championship is already coming into sharp focus. Ferrari narrowly leads the constructors' standings. Eetu Väisänen dominantingly leads the drivers'. The rest of the grid is scrambling for answers.

The Ferrari Picture

What has been most striking about Ferrari's start is not the wins themselves, but the immense variety of conditions in which they have reliably arrived. Melbourne was a treacherous full-wet race, deeply chaotic and completely reshuffled by dramatic punctures and multiple safety cars. Shanghai was the exact opposite: perfectly dry, deeply strategic, entirely built around tyre offset and precision pit timing. Two completely different endurance tests. The exact same dominant result.

The enormous complication, of course, is Afonso Cachinho. Ferrari's heavily scrutinized number two driver has shockingly not scored a single point in either race, has been severely penalised in both sessions of the Chinese weekend, and now precariously sits on six penalty points for the season—along with a formal warning from the n4x stewards already blotted on his record. If Ferrari hopes to maintain their constructor lead, Cachinho desperately needs a clean weekend.

The Challengers and Japan

Quarter Kevu remains the most credible, ever-present threat to Väisänen, and the paddock knows it. The reigning world champion has been relentlessly consistent without being outright dominant. Jason Roy and Red Bull are currently the most incredibly volatile element in the early standings, displaying massive raw pace but lacking the race-day flawless execution of Ferrari. Mercedes came into 2026 carrying genuine optimism after a highly strong end to 2025, and Wesley Vandesteene's incredible Melbourne second place felt like absolute confirmation that the team had arrived as a winning force rather than a mere contender-in-waiting.

Suzuka is a driver's circuit in the absolute truest sense: terrifyingly high-speed, utterly unforgiving, and technically demanding in a way that paper simulation performance numbers don't always capture. The deeply anticipated Japanese GP weekend runs from March 27th to 29th, giving teams just enough time to regroup for the challenge. Ferrari have both the aero efficiency and driver to win. Red Bull have heavily shown they can qualify on the front row absolutely anywhere. Whether the next seventeen rounds can organically deliver on the massive promise of that early drama is another thrilling question entirely. The core ingredients, at least, are perfectly in place.

Two rounds in, the championship is already delivering on its pre-season promise. How long Ferrari can sustain this is the question the rest of the paddock is paying to answer.

As the intense season progresses, absolute reliability and flawless operational execution on the pit wall will easily become just as vital as pure qualifying speed. Every single championship point is fiercely contested, and the margins for error have vanished completely. The teams that successfully manage internal pressure while continuously pushing aerodynamic development boundaries will undoubtedly be the ones standing triumphantly on the top step of the podium when the season inevitably reaches its dramatic conclusion.
